content:
# Acts Chapter 13 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 240-242 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_013_page_001.jpg, ACT_013_page_002.jpg, ACT_013_page_003.jpg

---

**1** Now there were in Antioch, in the church that was there, prophets and teachers: Barnabas, and Simeon who was called Niger, and Lucius the Cyrenian, and Manaen who had been brought up with Herod the tetrarch, and Saul.

**2** And as they were ministering to the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, "Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them."

**3** Then having fasted and prayed and laid their hands on them, they sent them away.

**4** So they, having been sent out by the Holy Spirit, went down to Seleucia, and from there they sailed to Cyprus.

**5** And when they were in Salamis, they proclaimed the word of God in the synagogues of the Jews. And they also had John as an attendant.

**6** And having gone through the whole island as far as Paphos, they found a certain man, a sorcerer, a Jewish false prophet, whose name was Bar-Jesus, **7** who was with the proconsul Sergius Paulus, an intelligent man. This man, having summoned Barnabas and Saul, sought to hear the word of God.

**8** But Elymas the sorcerer -- for so his name is translated -- opposed them, seeking to turn the proconsul away from the faith.

**9** But Saul, who is also Paul, having been filled with the Holy Spirit, looked intently at him **10** and said, "O you who are full of all deceit and all villainy, son of the devil, enemy of all righteousness, will you not cease perverting the straight ways of the Lord? **11** And now, behold, the hand of the Lord is upon you, and you shall be blind, not seeing the sun for a time." And immediately mist and darkness fell upon him, and going about he sought those who would lead him by the hand.

**12** Then the proconsul, having seen what had happened, believed, being astonished at the teaching of the Lord.

**13** And having set sail from Paphos, those around Paul came to Perga of Pamphylia. But John, having departed from them, returned to Jerusalem.

**14** And they, having passed through from Perga, came to Antioch of Pisidia, and having gone into the synagogue on the day of the Sabbath, they sat down.

**15** And after the reading of the Law and the Prophets, the rulers of the synagogue sent to them, saying, "Men, brothers, if there is in you any word of exhortation for the people, speak."

**16** And Paul, having stood up and motioning with his hand, said: "Men of Israel, and you who fear God, listen.

**17** The God of this people Israel chose our fathers and exalted the people during their sojourn in the land of Egypt, and with an uplifted arm he led them out of it.

**18** And for a period of about forty years he bore with them in the wilderness.

**19** And having destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, he gave them their land as an inheritance

**20** for about four hundred and fifty years. And after these things he gave them judges until Samuel the prophet.

**21** And from that time they asked for a king, and God gave them Saul the son of Kish, a man of the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years.

**22** And having removed him, he raised up David to be their king, to whom he also said, bearing witness, 'I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after my heart, who will do all my will.'

**23** From this man's seed, God, according to promise, brought to Israel a Savior, Jesus,

**24** after John had first proclaimed before the face of his coming a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.

**25** And as John was completing his course, he said, 'What do you suppose that I am? I am not he. But behold, one comes after me, the sandals of whose feet I am not worthy to loose.'

**26** Men, brothers, sons of the race of Abraham, and those among you who fear God, to us the word of this salvation has been sent.

**27** For those who dwell in Jerusalem and their rulers, not having known him, and the voices of the prophets that are read every Sabbath, fulfilled them by condemning him.

**28** And though they found no cause of death, they asked Pilate that he should be put to death.

**29** And when they had fulfilled all the things that had been written concerning him, they took him down from the tree and laid him in a tomb.

**30** But God raised him from the dead,

**31** and he appeared for many days to those who had come up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are now his witnesses to the people.

**32** And we proclaim to you the good news of the promise made to the fathers,

**33** that God has fulfilled this to us, their children, having raised up Jesus, as it is also written in the second Psalm: 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you.'

**34** And that he raised him from the dead, no longer about to return to corruption, he has spoken thus: 'I will give you the holy and sure things of David.'

**35** Therefore he also says in another place, 'You will not allow your Holy One to see corruption.'

**36** For David, having served the purpose of God in his own generation, fell asleep and was laid with his fathers and saw corruption,

**37** but he whom God raised up did not see corruption.

**38** Therefore let it be known to you, men and brothers, that through this one forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you,

**39** and from all things from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses, in this one everyone who believes is justified.

**40** Therefore take heed, lest that which was spoken in the prophets should come upon you:

**41** 'Look, you scoffers, and marvel, and perish, for I am working a work in your days, a work which you will by no means believe, even if someone declares it to you.'"

**42** And as they were going out, they urged that these words might be spoken to them on the next Sabbath.

**43** And when the synagogue had been dismissed, many of the Jews and the devout proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas, who, speaking to them, were persuading them to continue in the grace of God.

**44** And on the coming Sabbath, nearly the whole city was gathered together to hear the word of God.

**45** But when the Jews saw the crowds, they were filled with jealousy and were contradicting the things spoken by Paul, blaspheming.

**46** And speaking boldly, Paul and Barnabas said, "It was necessary that the word of God should be spoken to you first. Since you thrust it away and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we turn to the Gentiles.

**47** For so the Lord has commanded us: 'I have set you as a light of the Gentiles, that you should be for salvation to the end of the earth.'"

**48** And hearing this, the Gentiles were rejoicing and glorifying the word of the Lord, and as many as had been appointed to eternal life believed.

**49** And the word of the Lord was being spread throughout the whole region.

**50** But the Jews stirred up the devout women of prominence and the leading men of the city, and raised up persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and cast them out from their borders.

**51** And they, having shaken off the dust of their feet against them, came to Iconium.

**52** And the disciples were being filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 13 marks a decisive turning point in the book of Acts -- the beginning of the Pauline mission to the wider world. The chapter moves from the church at Antioch commissioning Barnabas and Saul (vv. 1-3), through the Cyprus ministry and the confrontation with Elymas (vv. 4-12), to the pivotal sermon at Pisidian Antioch (vv. 13-41) and its explosive aftermath (vv. 42-52). Three structural developments shape the entire remainder of Acts: the emergence of Paul as the mission's leader (v. 13, "those around Paul"), the Saul-to-Paul name transition (v. 9), and the programmatic turn from Jewish synagogue to Gentile audience (v. 46). Paul's synagogue sermon is the fullest example of his preaching to a Jewish audience, and its structure -- historical recital (vv. 17-22), christological proclamation (vv. 23-37), and call to decision (vv. 38-41) -- mirrors Stephen's speech in Acts 7 while reaching a fundamentally different conclusion: not judgment upon Israel but the offer of justification by faith.

**Manuscript condition:** Acts 13 begins in the fourth column of page 240 (folio 120r) and continues across all four columns of pages 241-242 (folios 120v-121r). The text is well-preserved throughout. Standard nomina sacra are used: ΘΣ/ΘΥ (God), ΚΣ/ΚΥ (Lord), ΙΣ/ΙΥ (Jesus), ΠΝΑ (Spirit). No significant lacunae affect the readings. The transition at verse 42 shows some minor wear but is legible.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show substantial agreement on the overall content and flow of this chapter. The primary points of divergence concern (1) the textual variant in v. 18 (etropophoresan vs. etrophophoresan -- "bore with" vs. "nourished"), (2) the psalm numbering in v. 33 ("second" vs. "first"), (3) the "word of God" vs. "word of the Lord" variant in v. 44, (4) the rendering of tetagmenoi in v. 48 ("appointed" vs. "destined"), (5) the handling of the Saul/Paul name transition at v. 9, and (6) several lexical choices throughout Paul's sermon (sorcerer vs. magician, villainy vs. fraud, corruption vs. decay).

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as the formal equivalence approach best serves Paul's extended sermon -- a piece of sustained theological rhetoric that demands precision. EMET's literal renderings inform key decisions where Greek morphology carries theological weight (the passive participle tetagmenoi in v. 48, the periphrastic construction in v. 18). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings are generally set aside in the sermon text, where precision is paramount, but PNEUMA's notes on the theological implications of key terms (especially v. 39 on justification and v. 48 on election) are valuable for understanding.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 1 -- "Simeon" vs. "Symeon":** EMET renders "Symeon" (preserving the Semitic form CYMEΩN); LOGOS and PNEUMA render "Simeon." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "Simeon" is the established English form. The Semitic original is noted.

2. **Verse 5 -- "attendant" vs. "minister" vs. "assistant":** EMET renders "attendant" (hypereten); LOGOS renders "minister"; PNEUMA renders "assistant." SOPHIA follows EMET: "attendant" best captures the subordinate helping role without the modern connotations of "minister" (which suggests clergy) or the casualness of "assistant."

3. **Verse 6 -- "sorcerer" vs. "magician":** EMET renders "sorcerer" (magon); LOGOS renders "magician"; PNEUMA renders "sorcerer." SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "sorcerer" carries the appropriate negative connotation that magos has in this context. "Magician" in modern English can suggest stage performance; Luke's intent is clearly pejorative.

4. **Verse 9 -- The Saul/Paul name transition:** EMET renders "Saul, the [one] also [called] Paul"; LOGOS renders "Saul, who is also Paul"; PNEUMA renders "Saul, who was also called Paul." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "Saul, who is also Paul" -- the present tense "is" (ho kai) reflects the simultaneity of the two names (Roman cognomen alongside Hebrew name) rather than a permanent change. From this verse forward, Luke uses "Paul" exclusively.

5. **Verse 10 -- "villainy" vs. "fraud":** EMET renders "villainy" (rhadiourgia); LOGOS renders "fraud"; PNEUMA renders "fraud and deception." The Greek rhadiourgia means reckless wickedness, unscrupulousness, or villainy -- it is broader and more severe than mere "fraud." SOPHIA follows EMET: "villainy" captures the scope of the term. PNEUMA's doubling ("fraud and deception") renders one Greek word as two English words.

6. **Verse 13 -- "those around Paul":** All three translators acknowledge the Greek hoi peri Paulon. EMET and LOGOS preserve the construction; PNEUMA renders "Paul and his companions." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "those around Paul" preserves the Lukan idiom that signals the shift in leadership from Barnabas to Paul. From this point, Paul is consistently named first.

7. **Verse 18 -- etropophoresan (bore with them) vs. etrophophoresan (nourished them):** This is a critical variant. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion. Sinaiticus reads ΕΤΡΟΠΟΦΟΡΗCΕΝ (etropophoresan, "he bore their manner/conduct"). SOPHIA renders "bore with them," following the Sinaiticus reading. The emphasis falls on God's patient endurance of Israel's waywardness in the wilderness, rather than on divine nurture.

8. **Verse 29 -- "tree" vs. "cross":** EMET and LOGOS render xylon as "tree"; PNEUMA renders "cross." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1): "tree" preserves the biblical resonance of xylon, which echoes Deuteronomy 21:22-23 ("cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree," quoted by Paul in Galatians 3:13). The word xylon means "wood" or "tree"; "cross" (stauros) is a different Greek word. The deliberate use of xylon in early Christian preaching carried theological freight that "cross" does not convey.

9. **Verse 33 -- "second Psalm":** All three translators read "second Psalm," consistent with the Sinaiticus reading ΔΕΥΤΕΡΩ. The variant "first Psalm" (ΠΡΩΤΩ) in some manuscripts reflects an ancient tradition where Psalms 1 and 2 were counted as a single psalm. SOPHIA follows the unanimous consensus: "the second Psalm." See the Disagreement Log for the variant discussion.

10. **Verse 33 -- "begotten" vs. "become your Father":** EMET and LOGOS render gegenneka as "I have begotten you"; PNEUMA renders "I have become your Father." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1): "begotten" preserves the language of Psalm 2:7 LXX and is the traditional rendering. PNEUMA's relational paraphrase captures the meaning but departs from the psalm's wording, which Paul is quoting directly.

11. **Verse 34 -- "corruption" vs. "decay":** EMET and LOGOS render diaphthora as "corruption"; PNEUMA renders "decay." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1): "corruption" is the established biblical English term for diaphthora in this context (the physical dissolution of the body), used consistently in Psalm 16:10 quotations across the KJV tradition. "Decay" is accurate but lacks the theological resonance.

12. **Verse 35 -- "another place" vs. "another psalm":** EMET renders "another [psalm]" (with "psalm" bracketed); LOGOS renders "another place"; PNEUMA renders "another place." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "another place" (en hetero) is appropriately general. The reference is to Psalm 16:10, and while "psalm" is contextually correct, the Greek says simply "in another."

13. **Verse 39 -- "justified" vs. "set free":** EMET and LOGOS render dikaiothēnai as "justified"; PNEUMA renders "set free." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1): "justified" preserves the forensic-legal sense of dikaioō that is central to Pauline theology. This verse is the earliest recorded instance of Paul's justification-by-faith proclamation in Acts and anticipates the full argument of Romans and Galatians. PNEUMA's "set free" captures the experiential dimension but obscures the juridical metaphor.

14. **Verse 41 -- "scoffers" vs. "despisers":** EMET renders "despisers" (kataphronetai); LOGOS and PNEUMA render "scoffers." The Greek kataphronetai (from kataphroneo, "to look down upon, despise") is rendered in the LXX of Habakkuk 1:5 and can mean either despisers or scoffers. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "scoffers" conveys both contempt and mockery and is more immediately vivid in English.

15. **Verse 44 -- "word of God" vs. "word of the Lord":** This is a significant manuscript variant. Sinaiticus reads TON ΛΟΓΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ("the word of God"). LOGOS reads "the word of the Lord," following the Byzantine text. EMET reads "the word of God," following Sinaiticus. PNEUMA reads "the word of the Lord." SOPHIA follows EMET and the Sinaiticus reading: "the word of God." See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

16. **Verse 46 -- "Gentiles" vs. "nations":** EMET renders ethne as "nations"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "Gentiles." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1): in this context, the contrast is explicitly between Jews and non-Jews, making "Gentiles" the clearer rendering. In the Old Testament quotation (v. 47), "Gentiles" is likewise used to match the context, though "nations" would also be defensible given the Isaiah 49:6 source.

17. **Verse 48 -- tetagmenoi ("appointed" vs. "destined"):** EMET renders "having been appointed"; LOGOS renders "had been appointed"; PNEUMA renders "destined." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "had been appointed." The perfect passive participle tetagmenoi preserves the divine passive -- these are people whom God had appointed. "Destined" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but shifts toward fate-language rather than the military/administrative ordering that tasso implies. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

18. **Verse 50 -- "worshipping women" vs. "devout women" vs. "God-fearing women":** EMET renders "worshipping women, the prominent [ones]"; LOGOS renders "devout women of prominence"; PNEUMA renders "prominent God-fearing women." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "devout women of prominence" captures the Greek tas sebomenas gynaikas tas euschemonas -- women who were both God-fearing proselytes and socially prominent.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "Symeon" vs. "Simeon" | "Symeon" | "Simeon" | "Simeon" | "Simeon" -- 2-to-1; established English form |
| 1 | "the existing church" vs. "the church that was there" vs. "the church at Antioch" | "in the existing church" | "in the church that was there" | "in the church at Antioch" | "in the church that was there" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the Greek participial construction (ten ousan ekklesian) |
| 1 | "foster-brother" vs. "brought up with" vs. "raised alongside" | "foster-brother" | "brought up with" | "raised alongside" | "brought up with" -- follows LOGOS; syntrophos means one reared together; "foster-brother" (EMET) is one specific meaning |
| 2 | "Set-apart indeed for me" vs. "Set apart for me" vs. "Set apart...for me" | "Set-apart indeed for me both" | "Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul" | "Set apart Barnabas and Saul for me" | "Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul" -- follows LOGOS; EMET's "indeed" (de) is a particle that need not be rendered emphatically |
| 3 | "they released [them]" vs. "they sent them away" vs. "sent them off" | "they released [them]" | "they sent them away" | "sent them off" | "they sent them away" -- follows LOGOS; apelusan means to release/send away; "sent away" captures the commissioning sense |
| 5 | "attendant" vs. "minister" vs. "assistant" | "attendant" | "minister" | "assistant" | "attendant" -- follows EMET; hyperetes = subordinate helper; see Council Notes |
| 6 | "sorcerer" vs. "magician" | "sorcerer" | "magician" | "sorcerer" | "sorcerer" -- 2-to-1; see Council Notes |
| 7 | "having summoned" vs. "summoned" vs. "sent for" | "having summoned" | "summoned" (in "This man summoned") | "sent for" | "having summoned" -- follows EMET; preserves the participial structure; SOPHIA adjusts to "This man, having summoned Barnabas and Saul, sought..." |
| 8 | "was opposing" vs. "opposed" | "was opposing" (imperfect) | "opposed" | "opposed" | "opposed" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); the aorist anthistato is rendered as simple past |
| 9 | Saul/Paul name transition phrasing | "the [one] also [called] Paul" | "who is also Paul" | "who was also called Paul" | "who is also Paul" -- follows LOGOS; present tense preserves simultaneity of names; see Council Notes |
| 9 | "having gazed-intently" vs. "looked intently" vs. "locked eyes" | "having gazed-intently" | "looked intently" | "locked eyes" | "looked intently" -- follows LOGOS; atenisas means to gaze intently; PNEUMA's "locked eyes" adds confrontational drama not in the verb itself |
| 10 | "villainy" vs. "fraud" vs. "fraud and deception" | "villainy" (rhadiourgia) | "fraud" | "fraud and deception" | "villainy" -- follows EMET; rhadiourgia is broader than "fraud"; see Council Notes |
| 11 | "there fell upon him a mist and a darkness" vs. "mist and darkness fell upon him" | "there fell upon him a mist and a darkness" | "mist and darkness fell upon him" | "a dark fog fell over him" | "mist and darkness fell upon him" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "dark fog" merges two distinct nouns (achlys + skotos) |
| 13 | "those around Paul" vs. "Paul and his companions" | "those around Paul" | "those around Paul" | "Paul and his companions" | "those around Paul" -- 2-to-1; preserves Lukan idiom marking leadership shift |
| 13 | "having been brought-out-to-sea" vs. "having set sail" vs. "set sail" | "having been brought-out-to-sea" | "having set sail" | "set sail" | "having set sail" -- follows LOGOS; anachthentes is a nautical term meaning "to put out to sea" |
| 14 | "Antioch the Pisidian" vs. "Antioch of Pisidia" vs. "Pisidian Antioch" | "Antioch the Pisidian" | "Antioch of Pisidia" | "Pisidian Antioch" | "Antioch of Pisidia" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek adjective Pisidian modifies Antioch; "of Pisidia" is the conventional English form |
| 14 | "the day of the sabbaths" vs. "the day of the Sabbath" vs. "the Sabbath day" | "the day of the sabbaths" | "the day of the Sabbath" | "the Sabbath day" | "the day of the Sabbath" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the Greek construction te hemera ton sabbaton |
| 16 | "Men, Israelites" vs. "Men of Israel" vs. "People of Israel" | "Men, Israelites" | "Men of Israel" | "People of Israel" | "Men of Israel" -- follows LOGOS; andres Israelitai is rendered naturally; PNEUMA's "People" removes the gender-specific address of the Greek |
| 17 | "the people he exalted" vs. "exalted the people" vs. "made the people great" | "the people he exalted" | "exalted the people" | "made the people great" | "exalted the people" -- follows LOGOS; hypsosen = exalted/lifted up; PNEUMA's "made great" dilutes the theological term |
| 17 | "uplifted arm" vs. "uplifted arm" vs. "mighty power" | "uplifted arm" | "uplifted arm" | "mighty power" | "uplifted arm" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); meta brachionos hypsElou = "with an uplifted/high arm"; preserves the biblical anthropomorphism from the Exodus tradition |
| **18** | **etropophoresan vs. etrophophoresan (MAJOR VARIANT)** | "bore-with-them" (etropophoresan) | "endured them" (etropophoresan) | "put up with them" (notes variant) | **"bore with them"** -- follows EMET; Sinaiticus reads ΕΤΡΟΠΟΦΟΡΗCΕΝ; see Council Notes and Disagreement Log |
| 20 | "And after these [things]" vs. "And after these things" vs. "After that" | "And after these [things]" | "And after these things" | "After that" | "And after these things" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "After that" loses the demonstrative |
| 20 | "the prophet" included | "Samuel the prophet" | "Samuel the prophet" | "Samuel the prophet" | "Samuel the prophet" -- unanimous |
| 22 | "desires" vs. "will" vs. "everything I want him to do" | "all my desires" (thelEmata) | "all my will" | "everything I want him to do" | "all my will" -- follows LOGOS; thelema = will/desire; "will" is more formal and traditional |
| 23 | "brought" vs. "brought" vs. "has brought" | "brought" (Egagen) | "brought" | "has brought" | "brought" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); aorist rendered as simple past in historical narration |
| 23 | "a savior, Jesus" vs. "a Savior, Jesus" | "a savior, Jesus" (lowercase) | "a Savior, Jesus" (capitalized) | "a Savior -- Jesus" (with dash) | "a Savior, Jesus" -- follows LOGOS; capitalized as a title |
| 24 | Sentence structure | "John having previously-proclaimed..." (genitive absolute) | "after John had first proclaimed..." (temporal clause) | "Before Jesus came, John had already been preaching..." | "after John had first proclaimed..." -- follows LOGOS; converts the Greek genitive absolute into a natural English temporal clause |
| 25 | "What do you suppose me to be?" vs. "What do you suppose that I am?" vs. "Who do you suppose I am?" | "What do you suppose me to be?" | "What do you suppose that I am?" | "Who do you suppose I am?" | "What do you suppose that I am?" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek ti (what) is interrogative, not "who" (PNEUMA) |
| 26 | "family" vs. "race" vs. "family" | "sons of [the] family of Abraham" | "sons of the race of Abraham" | "children of Abraham's family" | "sons of the race of Abraham" -- follows LOGOS; genous = race/stock/lineage; "race" is more precise for genos |
| 27 | Sentence structure (complex verse) | Literal participial chain | "not having known him...fulfilled them by condemning him" | "did not recognize him. By condemning him, they fulfilled..." | "not having known him...fulfilled them by condemning him" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the ironic structure where the participles of ignorance and condemnation lead to fulfillment |
| 28 | "no cause of death" vs. "no cause of death" vs. "no legitimate grounds for a death sentence" | "no cause of death" | "no cause of death" | "no legitimate grounds for a death sentence" | "no cause of death" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); PNEUMA's expansion is interpretive |
| 29 | **"tree" vs. "cross"** | "the tree" (xylon) | "the tree" | "the cross" | **"the tree"** -- 2-to-1; xylon = wood/tree; see Council Notes |
| 32 | "bringing-good-news" vs. "proclaim...the good news" vs. "bringing you the good news" | "we are bringing-good-news to you" | "we proclaim to you the good news" | "we are bringing you the good news" | "we proclaim to you the good news" -- follows LOGOS; euangelizometha rendered as "proclaim the good news" |
| **33** | **"second psalm" (ΔΕΥΤΕΡΩ) -- variant discussion** | "the second psalm" | "the second Psalm" | "the second Psalm" | **"the second Psalm"** -- unanimous; Sinaiticus reads ΔΕΥΤΕΡΩ; see Council Notes |
| 33 | "I have begotten you" vs. "I have become your Father" | "I have begotten you" | "I have begotten you" | "I have become your Father" | "I have begotten you" -- 2-to-1; preserves Psalm 2:7 LXX language; see Council Notes |
| 33 | "the children of them" vs. "their children" vs. "us, their children" | "to us, the children of them" | "to us, their children" | "for us, their children" | "to us, their children" -- follows LOGOS; the Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΙC ΤΕΚΝΟΙC ΑΥΤΩΝ |
| 34 | "the holy [things] of David, the faithful [things]" vs. "the holy and sure things of David" vs. "the holy and sure blessings promised to David" | "the holy [things] of David, the faithful [things]" | "the holy and sure things of David" | "the holy and sure blessings promised to David" | "the holy and sure things of David" -- follows LOGOS; ta hosia Dauid ta pista; PNEUMA adds "blessings promised" which is interpretive |
| 34 | "corruption" vs. "corruption" vs. "decay" | "corruption" | "corruption" | "decay" | "corruption" -- 2-to-1; see Council Notes |
| 35 | "another [psalm]" vs. "another place" | "another [psalm]" | "another place" | "another place" | "another place" -- 2-to-1; en hetero is general |
| 36 | "counsel" vs. "purpose" vs. "purpose" | "the counsel of God" (boulE) | "the purpose of God" | "God's purpose" | "the purpose of God" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); boulE can mean counsel or purpose; "purpose" captures the intentional plan of God |
| 36 | "was added to his fathers" vs. "was laid with his fathers" vs. "was buried with his ancestors" | "was added to his fathers" | "was laid with his fathers" | "was buried with his ancestors" | "was laid with his fathers" -- follows LOGOS; prosetethE pros tous pateras can mean "was gathered/added to"; "laid with" is natural English for burial; PNEUMA's "ancestors" loses the more intimate "fathers" |
| 38 | "men, brothers" vs. "men and brothers" vs. "brothers" | "men, brothers" | "men and brothers" | "brothers" | "men and brothers" -- follows LOGOS; andres adelphoi rendered naturally; PNEUMA drops andres |
| 39 | **"justified" vs. "set free"** | "is justified" | "is justified" | "is set free" | **"is justified"** -- 2-to-1; dikaioō is forensic; see Council Notes |
| 40 | "Watch" vs. "take heed" vs. "be careful" | "Watch therefore" | "Therefore take heed" | "So be careful" | "Therefore take heed" -- follows LOGOS; blepete = watch/take heed; "take heed" conveys the gravity of the prophetic warning |
| 41 | "despisers" vs. "scoffers" vs. "scoffers" | "the despisers" | "you scoffers" | "you scoffers" | "you scoffers" -- 2-to-1; see Council Notes |
| 42 | "they were urging" vs. "they urged" vs. "the people begged them" | "they were urging" | "they urged" | "the people begged them" | "they urged" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA identifies the subject as "the people" and intensifies to "begged," both interpretive |
| 43 | "worshipping proselytes" vs. "devout proselytes" vs. "devout converts to Judaism" | "the worshipping proselytes" | "the devout proselytes" | "devout converts to Judaism" | "the devout proselytes" -- follows LOGOS; sebomenon proselyton; PNEUMA's expansion "converts to Judaism" explains but is not in the text |
| **44** | **"word of God" (ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ) vs. "word of the Lord" (ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ)** | "the word of God" (Sinaiticus) | "the word of the Lord" (Byzantine) | "the word of the Lord" | **"the word of God"** -- follows EMET and Sinaiticus; see Council Notes and Disagreement Log |
| 45 | "contradicting and blaspheming" vs. "contradicting...blaspheming" | "contradicting and blaspheming" (two actions) | "contradicting...blaspheming" | "contradicting...hurling insults" | "contradicting...blaspheming" -- follows LOGOS; blasphemountes = blaspheming; PNEUMA's "hurling insults" weakens the term |
| 46 | "thrusting it away" vs. "thrust it away" vs. "pushing it aside" | "are thrusting it away" | "thrust it away" | "are pushing it aside" | "thrust it away" -- follows LOGOS; apotheisthe = thrust away/reject |

| 49 | "was being carried" vs. "was being spread" vs. "spread" | "was being carried through" (diephereto) | "was being spread throughout" | "spread through" | "was being spread throughout" -- follows LOGOS; the imperfect passive conveys ongoing dissemination |

| 51 | "having shaken-off the dust of the feet" vs. "having shaken off the dust of their feet" | "the dust of the feet" | "the dust of their feet" | "the dust from their feet" | "the dust of their feet" -- follows LOGOS; possessive "their" is natural English |
